text:
and how about connection limiter needing to turn off? does it ever get to a state where it turns off completely again? or once on, never turns off?
also not sure what it means in the formular if any rate limiting happened, you are saying that rejected connections make limit to relax? but isnt it the point of the connection rate limiter.. to limit conne
ctions? interesting
that the
INCREASE part of the formula doesn't care about connection rate, just the rejections
• Great questions! Let me address both.
1. Does it ever turn OFF?
Looking at the state machine:
Inactive → WaitForWarmup → NeedsEnablement → EnabledAtDefaultRate
EnabledAtCustomRate
• (loops forever)
There's no path back to disabled. The philosophy seems to be:
- Always-on protection - rate limiter is
a safety net
- Rate goes UP when things are healthy (effectively "unlimited" at high rates)
- But limiter stays enabled in case of sudden connection storm
No - once enabled, it stays enabled forever. The rate can increase indefinitely, but ingressConnectionEstablishmentRateLimiterEnabled is never set back to false.
2. Why does INCREASE care about rejections, not connection rate?
You're right to find this interesting! The logic is:
if rate_limiting_detected && cpu_overload == CPUOverload: : None {
// INCREASE rate limit
Translation: "If we're actively rejecting connections, but CPU has plenty of headroom, then our rate limit is too strict."
The reasoning:
Situation
Rate limit too strict
Rate limit appropriate
Under attack
Healthy load
CPU
Idle ≥ 20%
Idle ≥ 20%
Idle < 10%
Idle < 10%
Rejections
Yes
No
Yes
No
Action
Increase
Nothing
Decrease
Nothing
Why
CPU can handle more, stop rejecting
Everything's fine
CPU overloaded, reject more
High CPU but not from connections
The key insight:
The rate limiter's job is to protect CPU from connection storms. If:
- Connections are being rejected (limiter is active)
- But CPU is chilling (idle ≥ 20%)
..then the limiter is being too aggressive. It's rejecting connections unnecessarily. So we relax it.
Contrast with DECREASE:
if cpu_overload == CPUOverload: : Severe d& elevated_connection_rate != ElevatedConnectionRate: :None {
// DECREASE rate limit
Here we DO care about connection rate - we only tighten if:
- CPU is crushed (idle < 10%)
- AND connections are coming in fast
This prevents tightening the limiter when CPU is busy for other reasons (queries, aggregations, etc.).
Summary
Direction
Decrease
Increase
Nothing
Nothing
Trigger
High CPU + High conn rate
Low CPU + Rejections happening
Low CPU + No rejections
High CPU + Low conn rate
Rationale
Connections are causing CPU pain
We're rejecting but don't need to
Healthy, limiter not active
CPU busy but not from connections
